---
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# (PART\*) Spatial Modelling {.unnumbered}

# Interpolation {#interpolation}

## Introduction

![](images/banner-interpolation.png)

### Aims {.unnumbered}

The aims of this practical are to:

1.  Introduce geostatistical approaches for analysing spatial data.
2.  Understand how common spatial interpolation techniques work.
3.  Perform interpolation in ArcGIS and become familiar with common
    applications e.g., for prediction, as an input for spatial
    regression.

### Application {.unnumbered}

To achieve this, we'll apply interpolation techniques to Point samples
of global plant trait data (leaf area, plant height), modelled by
@lusk2026. Our study area is Peru, which features extreme gradients in
climate and topography, which are likely to be reflected in spatial
gradients in plant traits.

### Data {.unnumbered}

This practical uses global plant trait data derived from crowdsourced
biodiversity monitoring and Earth observation, available in
[`data/global-plant-traits`]
[[Paper](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-68996-y)]
[[Source](https://global-traits.projects.earthengine.app/view/global-traits)]

### Tools {.unnumbered}

[Extract Values to
Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/extract-values-to-points.html?tabs=dialog)
(Spatial Analyst Tools),
[IDW](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geostatistical-analyst/idw.html?tabs=dialog)
(Geostatistical Analyst Tools), [Empirical Bayesian
Kriging](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geostatistical-analyst/empirical-bayesian-kriging.html?tabs=dialog)
(Geostatistical Analyst Tools), [Geostatistical
Wizard](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/get-started-with-geostatistical-analyst-in-arcgis-pro.html#9E3),
[Exploratory
Interpolation](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geostatistical-analyst/exploratory-interpolation.html?tabs=dialog)
(Geostatistical Analyst Tools)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical

[**!!!**] Problem: the semivariogram for 100 random points is flat (too sparse) - which is "correct" but is not communicating the key message ... not super clear with n = 400

[A] Extract Spain and Iberia
[B] Multipart to Singlepart
[C] Calculate geometry attributes (Area Geodesic)
[D] Delete < 80000 (km2)
[E] Project (custom, conformal)
[F] Create Random Points (using Iberia as a constaint)

... Perhaps Peru too stark over a short area? (most sampling points are the same, except near the coast)

No obvious improvement...

The sparse random sampling may be the problem.

Solution? 

[A] Random Points within Iberia
[B] Supplement with additional random points with smaller areas e.g., nature reserves, provinces, national parks? https://www.miteco.gob.es/en/biodiversidad/servicios/banco-datos-naturaleza/informacion-disponible/enp_descargas.html

Spin a yarn - this sampling distribution is more representative of what we might see for other ecological datasets i.e., reasonable point coverage overall, but clusters in beautiful, popular, accessible spots...

OR

go back to the drawing board and find a point dataset

Source: https://peru.mapbiomas.org/en/capas/

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Interpolation techniques are used to estimate unknown values at new
locations based on known measurements from surrounding sample points.

We could use our species observation Points (*Panthera tigris*) from
[Practical 5](#point_patterns) here, but it's not an ideal example, for
a few reasons:

1.  Our Points don't have corresponding `values` that could be used for
    spatial interpolation e.g., animal height, age, or mass for each
    observation.

2.  As our Points represent individual observations, we would typically
    have to aggregate these data in some way to perform interpolation
    i.e., either via
    [tessellation](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/generatetesellation.html?tabs=dialog)
    to a grid or by generating a new Point dataset, where the `value`
    represents the number of observations within a specified distance.

3.  As demonstrated in [Practical 5](#point_patterns), the distribution
    of *Panthera tigris* observations is highly clustered and
    non-continuous, with observations primarily in designated nature
    reserves. Interpolation techniques will typically struggle to
    capture this spatial pattern, and instead, we'd probably want to use
    a *species distribution model* (an example of a *point-process
    model*) in this situation. You can learn about these in [Spatial
    Ecology](https://www.manchester.ac.uk/study/masters/courses/list/07053/msc-geographical-information-science/)
    in Semester 2.

4.  Finally, our species observations are *Presence-Only* data (i.e.,
    recorded observations of *Panthera tigris*), and we have no access
    to corresponding *Absence-data*, which you can read about more fully
    in @grattarola2023 and @kent2011. As a result, we do not know
    whether the "gaps" in our [Point-pattern](#p5_randomness) analysis
    reflects true absence, or simply sampling bias [@hughes2021].

**Instead**, we are going to apply interpolation to a dataset where we
know the spatial pattern is continuous, and where we have a variable we
want to predict.

### Pre-processing

[1] Load leaf area raster [`3117-leaf-area-peru.tif`] and Natural-Earth
dataset [`ne_10m_admin_0_countries.shp`]

[2] Generate 100 random points, within bounding geometry of Peru,
excluding marine-areas:

-   Select by Attributes: Peru
-   [Minimum Bounding
    Geometry](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/minimum-bounding-geometry.html?tabs=dialog)
    (envelope)
-   [Clip](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/clip.html?tabs=dialog)
    output to Peru geometry
-   [Create Random
    Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/create-random-points.html?tabs=dialog),
    n=100, constrain to geometry

> This dataset represents the sampling locations we will use for
> interpolation.

Alterantively - tesselate to create grid (3000 square kilmeters) for systematic sampling


[3] [Extract Values to
Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/extract-values-to-points.html?tabs=dialog),
using the leaf are raster as input.

> Each location now has a corresponding value (SLA, specifc leaf area index)

[4] Remove any rows where values are invalid (-9999) i.e., lakes or
rivers.

-   Select by Attribute (RASTERVALU \<\> -9999, Export selection)

[5] We now have a valid dataset (all points within bounding geometry, on
land, with a SLA value). However, we are again performing geometry
calculations (i.e., predicting values at locations), so we need to
working with data that is suitably projected, rather than using a
geographic CRS.

-   Use
    [Project](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/project.html?tabs=dialog)
    and apply a suitable CRS

> Peru96 UTM zone 18S ([EPSG:5387](https://epsg.io/5387)) is a good
> choice here.

### Interpolation types

> Discuss **deterministic** and **geostatistical** approaches.

> Briefly introduce the history of **econometrics** (Practicals 2 - 5,
> 7 - 8) and **geostatistics** (Practical 6)

> Discuss **global** and **local** interpolators.

> Discuss **exact** and **approximate** interpolators.

The ArcGIS Pro
[documentation](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/deterministic-methods-for-spatial-interpolation.html)
is a useful overview, including for individual tools e.g.,
[IDW](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/how-inverse-distance-weighted-interpolation-works.html)

@heusler2025 and @rufino2021 are useful ecological examples.

### Deterministic interpolation

[6] We'll begin with a simple interpolation method, inverse distance
weighting, via
[IDW](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/idw.html?tabs=dialog):

-   Input: Sample points
-   Z value field: Leaf area
-   Output raster: *suitable name*
-   Output cell size: default (units of CRS, m)
-   Leave all inputs as default, which we'll explore further later on
    (Power, Search neighborhood, \# neighbors, sector type)

> TO DO: include IDW equation and explanation.

[7] Interpret outputs:

-   IDW produces predictions within the bounds of the input data, hence
    *interpolation*, rather than *extrapolation*
-   Your output will differ from mine, given the generation of random
    sample points in [2], but broadly it is capturing the expected
    gradients in specific leaf area, as a function of climate.

[8] The
[IDW](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/idw.html?tabs=dialog)
tool used above has produced a static layer, and we could re-run the
tool with different settings to explore their impact on the predicted
surface. We can achieve this much more simply, however, via the
[Geostatistical
Wizard](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/get-started-with-geostatistical-analyst-in-arcgis-pro.html#9E3).
This tool includes a range of deterministic and geostatistical
interpolation methods and guides the user as they construct and evaluate
the performance of an interpolation model.

As a result, while all of the interpolation methods can be run in a
standalone tool, such as
[IDW](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/idw.html?tabs=dialog)
or [Empirical Bayesian
Kriging](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geostatistical-analyst/empirical-bayesian-kriging.html?tabs=dialog),
the Geostatistical Wizard can offer a more streamlined and dynamic
experience.

[9] First run with the [Geostatistical
Wizard](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/get-started-with-geostatistical-analyst-in-arcgis-pro.html#9E3):

-   Select Deterministic Methods
-   Inverse Distance Weighting
-   Source Dataset and Data Field
-   Leave `Weight Field` blank: this allows us to weight each
    observation i.e., some Points will have more / less impact on the
    output surface

The output should be identical to the
[IDW](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/idw.html?tabs=dialog)
output, but we can check this:

-   Select Finish to export the layer to the Map
-   This is a Shapefile Feature Class, so we need to Export Layer - To
    Rasters
-   We can then use [Extract Multi Values to
    Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/extract-multi-values-to-points.html?tabs=dialog)
    to sample both rasters (one created by IDW and one by Geostatistical
    Wizard) at our original Point locations
-   Create Chart - Scatter Plot - R2

> **James** can you reproduce this? The outputs of IDW and the
> Geostatistical Wizard don't show systematic error (R2=1), but there
> are deviations for most points, mean difference of 0.06, max = 0.398

[10] Open the [Geostatistical
Wizard](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/get-started-with-geostatistical-analyst-in-arcgis-pro.html#9E3)
where we'll explore the key properties.

> Click on the map to identify the predicted value (for the current
> settings)

> Change Point size for visibility and toggle 'Show Source Dataset' to
> identify the Points which are contributing to the predicted value at
> the current location, where the colour represents the weight (inverse
> distance)

> If we modify 'Maximum Nieghbours', we can see how this increases the
> size of the 'Source Dataset'. Note that once the maximum reaches \~50,
> the number of selected neighbours stops increasing, presumably because
> ArcGIS curtails this process once they have a negligble impact on the
> output. **TO DO** find documentation to support this.

> We can also change 'Sector Type'. Default = 1 i.e., we will look for
> nearest N neighbours within that area. If we increase to 4 or 8
> sectors (and modify their `Angle`), we look for N neighbours per
> sector.

> An important setting is **power** which determines the influence of
> the input points on the prediction, as a function of distance. The
> default is 2 i.e., $distance^2$. As we increase this parameter, closer
> points play an increasingly important role in the output. Compare
> power = 1 - 10.

> Power can also be optimised, which is via *root-mean-square cross
> validation* which we'll discuss more fully in the next section.

[11] Cross validation of IDW

On the next tab, we are presenting with *predicted* values, *errors*,
and the *distribution* of the predicted values.

These are derived from
[cross-validation](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/performing-cross-validation-and-validation.html), which involves
removing a single point from the input dataset, re-running
interpolation, and then evaluating the deviation of the predicted value
from the input value. This is repeated for all points in the input
dataset.

This can be used to calculated root-mean-square error (RMSE):

$$\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n(O_i-M_i)^2} $$ which is the square root of the sum
($\sum$) of the squared differences between observed values ($O_i$,
*input specific leaf area*) and modelled values ($M_i$, *interpolated
specific leaf area*). This is the average distance between the model
predictions and the observed values and is expressed in the units of the
observed values (i.e., SLA).

> Interpret the RMSE value (\~2.018) and the correspondence between
> observed and modelled

> Inspect the pattern of error - is this systematic? (X = measured
> values, Y = error)

> Inspect the distribution of values

### Geostatistical interpolation

> To start, we'll discussing the origin of geostatistics and the key differences with deterministic methods i.e., incorporating the spatial structure of the data (*variography*, spatial autocorrelation). As summarised in the [documentation](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/kriging-in-geostatistical-analyst.html): "*Some of the spatial variation ... can be modeled by random processes with spatial autocorrelation, and require that the spatial autocorrelation be explicitly modeled*."

**Kriging**, developed and popularised by @krige1951 and @matheron1963, is synonymous with the field of geostatistics, and comes in [many versions](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/what-are-the-different-kriging-models-.html), including Ordinary, Universal, Co-kriging, Indicator, and Empirical Bayesian, some of which we'll use today. Understanding how these work, and selecting the appropriate version for your data and analysis, is therefore critical. 

ESRI [video](https://mediaspace.esri.com/media/t/1_tc82hmoa)

Youtube [walkthrough](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-u4fJJfUGjw)

#### Semivariograms

One of the key components of all forms of Kriging is the semivariogram, which measures the difference between data points at specific distances i.e., the similarity of data points (y) with distance (x).

> Include schematic [example](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/creating-empirical-semivariograms.html) here

> Include variogram example here and explain key components (range, nugget, sill)

#### Ordinary Kriging {.unnumbered}

The assumes the data has a constant, but unknown mean (*stationarity*)

[12] Ordinary Kriging via the Geostatistical Wizard, although this can also be acheived via [Kriging](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/kriging.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Analyst Tools).

- In the former, selecting Kriging / CoKriging
- For each Kriging method, we can generate a range of [outputs](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/what-output-surface-types-can-the-interpolation-models-generate-.html):
  - *Prediction* i.e., the interpolated values (default)
  - *Standard Error* i.e., errors associated with those interpolated values (standard deviation)
  - *Probability* i.e., the probability that the interpolated value will be above or below a predefined threshold (e.g., pollutant level)
  - *Quantile* i.e., a layer for the specified quantile (e.g., 10^th^, 95^th^)
- Determine Transformation type (None)
- Determine Order of Trend Removal (None)

This produces the semivariogram for our dataset.

[Fitting to the empirical semivariogram](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/fitting-a-model-to-the-empirical-semivariogram.html)

[Comparison paper](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoinf.2010.12.003)

#### Universal Kriging {.unnumbered}

Assumes the data mean is not constant, but varies gradually across space according to a mathematical function (*non-stationarity*). We should use this approach because we *would* expect non-stationarity i.e., trends in climate, elevation...

#### Empirical Bayesian Kriging {.unnumbered}

Accounts for error in estimating the semivariogram. 

## Extra

In the practical we've used *cross-validation* to assess interpolation quality. We can also assess the quality of the interpolation using an independent set of validation locations. This can be achieved by: 

- Generating a set of validation locations via [Create Random Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/create-random-points.html?tabs=dialog)
- Extracting the corresponding values from the input data and the interpolated layers using [Extract Multi Values to Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/extract-multi-values-to-points.html?tabs=dialog)
- Create Chart - Scatter Plot - R2

> This approach is only possible because our analysis is based on a continuous input surface [`3117-leaf-area-peru.tif`]. Typically, interpolation of Point data would be used to *create* a continuous surface - if we have it already, we wouldn't need to interpolate!

> We also need to evalute performance cautiously, acknowledging that the input is also a [model](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-68996-y).

## Resources

-   Krige, D.G. ([1951](https://hdl.handle.net/10520/AJA0038223X_4792))
    A statistical approach to some basic mine valuation problems on the
    Witwatersrand. J Chem Metal Min Soc S Afr, December, 119–139
-   Matheron, G. ([1963b](https://doi.org/10.2113/gsecongeo.58.8.1246))
    Principles of geostatistics. Econ Geol 58(8):1246–1266
-   Oliver, M.A. and Webster, R.
    ([1990](https://doi.org/10.1080/02693799008941549)). Kriging: a
    method of interpolation for geographical information systems.
    International Journal of Geographical Information System, 4(3),
    pp.313-332.
-   Diggle, P.J., Tawn, J.A. and Moyeed, R.A.
    ([1998](https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9876.00113)). Model-based
    geostatistics. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series C:
    Applied Statistics, 47(3), pp.299-350.
-   Rufino, M.M., Albouy, C. and Brind'Amour, A.
    ([2021](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2021.109501)). Which
    spatial interpolators I should use? A case study applying to marine
    species. Ecological Modelling[MT3.1], 449, p.109501.
